_note: Per-specialist case material for Legal Awareness. Used at workerNode (per specialty turn), not at recommendation. The specialist retrieves 2-3 cases whose `business_type_category` matches the inferred category, and weaves the legal-blindspot insight into the turn without naming the source — per CLAUDE.md GR#6 ("use the case, don't cite it"). Every case here exhibits Legal's domain discipline: name a specific category of legal exposure this owner is about to walk into, calibrate the urgency (before launch / before signing / before hire / worth addressing later / talk to a lawyer today), and specify what kind of lawyer the owner would need. Legal Awareness does NOT give legal advice; it flags the category so the owner knows what they don't know.

cases.challenge_pattern: consultant with handshake scope arrangements that expand over the engagement, leading to disputes that contracts would have prevented
cases.observation: Independent consultant in her second year with two client disputes in twelve months. Both stemmed from scope that had drifted: client had originally engaged for 'strategic advisory' at a retainer; over six months the work had expanded to include implementation support, then project management of another vendor, then operational documentation — none of it captured in writing. When disputes came (one about deliverables, one about payment for out-of-scope work), there was no contract that defined what scope had been. She ate the loss on one; the other required small-claims court. The legal category was 'professional services contract hygiene' — a urgency-medium thing that becomes urgency-today once a dispute lands.
cases.what_worked: Flagged the category, not as a panic but as mechanics: 'you need a standard engagement letter that names the scope, the hourly or retainer structure, what's in and what's out, how scope changes are handled, and what happens at termination. A business attorney familiar with professional-services contracts can draft the template for $800-2500 and it'll last years.' Calibrated urgency as 'before your next new engagement.' Consultant engaged a local business attorney, got the template, and two years later the template had prevented at least four scope-creep arguments.
cases.what_wasted_money: Two quarters of legal exposure and roughly $7-12K in billable work she couldn't collect on the out-of-scope disputes. The template cost a fraction of one loss.
cases.one_line_lesson: Handshake scope is a dispute waiting to happen. A standard engagement letter with scope, rates, changes, and termination clauses is table stakes for professional services — not a nice-to-have.

cases.challenge_pattern: firm hiring an experienced professional from a direct competitor where the new hire has a non-compete they haven't mentioned
cases.observation: Growing therapy practice recruiting an experienced clinician from a larger practice across town. Onboarding call, the new clinician mentioned 'I had a non-compete at my last place, not sure if it's enforceable.' The owner-hiring hadn't asked and was about to bring her onboard. The legal category: non-compete enforceability varies heavily by state (some states void most non-competes, others enforce them if reasonable in scope and duration); in healthcare and therapy specifically, some jurisdictions have specific restrictions on clinician non-competes, and client-following rules can be different from employee-following rules. This is lawyer territory before the hire is finalized, not after.
cases.what_worked: Flagged urgency-before-signing: 'this is a talk-to-an-employment-lawyer-this-week situation before you finalize the offer. The question isn't whether she can join you — it's whether she can legally bring or accept referrals from former clients in the first X months, and whether your practice could be sued for tortious interference if she does. Non-compete enforceability in this state for therapists is specific enough that a licensed employment attorney needs to read the actual document.' Owner paused hiring, got the non-compete reviewed by an employment lawyer ($400 consultation); found it was partially enforceable for 9 months on direct-referral-following but not on net-new clients. Structured the hire accordingly; avoided a lawsuit from the prior practice.
cases.what_wasted_money: Would have been legal fees plus potential settlement if the prior practice had sued for clinician-following. Prior-practice lawsuits against former-employee non-competes are not rare in healthcare.
cases.one_line_lesson: Non-competes are state-specific, profession-specific, and often have nuance around what's enforced vs. what's just on paper. A $400 attorney review before finalizing a hire from a competitor is cheap insurance against a much larger lawsuit.

cases.challenge_pattern: service business with a generic internet-downloaded waiver that doesn't meet state-specific enforceability requirements
cases.observation: Small adventure-sports outfitter (guided rock climbing) with a waiver they'd downloaded from a template site and had every client sign. Clear and simple, but it had issues the owner didn't know about: (1) the state required specific language about 'gross negligence' for the waiver to cover anything significant; (2) the font size was below the state's enforceability floor; (3) waivers for minors required the parent/guardian to sign AND a specific additional paragraph that wasn't there. If an injury had required litigation, the waiver was likely to be only partially enforceable and the owner's liability exposure far exceeded what she thought she was carrying.
cases.what_worked: Named urgency-before-next-season: 'adventure-sports waivers are heavily state-regulated, and generic templates don't meet the requirements. You need a waiver reviewed or drafted by a lawyer who handles outdoor-recreation liability in this state. Budget $500-1500 for the review; factor in separate language for minors. Also confirm your liability insurance policy — some policies require specific waiver language to provide coverage, and a waiver your insurer won't back is worse than none.' Owner engaged a specialist attorney, got a properly-drafted waiver, updated her intake flow. An injury two years later was covered by insurance and the waiver held.
cases.what_wasted_money: Would have been the full liability exposure of an uninsured claim if the generic waiver had failed in court. One five-figure injury claim could have closed the business.
cases.one_line_lesson: Generic template waivers often don't meet state-specific enforceability requirements, and an unenforceable waiver creates a false sense of protection. For high-liability activities, pay for a proper attorney-drafted waiver — it's lower than the cost of one disputed claim.

cases.challenge_pattern: contractor whose license category doesn't cover the work they're about to start doing
cases.observation: Residential landscaper adding hardscaping (patios, retaining walls, small drainage work). In his state, hardscaping above certain scope crossed into 'general contractor' or 'masonry contractor' license territory — not covered by his landscape license. Doing unlicensed work in those categories would have: (a) voided his liability insurance on any hardscape job, (b) created license-board penalties, (c) made any client dispute essentially indefensible (no lien rights, possible contract voiding). He didn't know. He'd been told by a peer 'just do it, nobody checks.'
cases.what_worked: Flagged urgency-before-launch-of-the-new-service: 'before you take a hardscape job, confirm which license category it falls under in this state and whether your current license covers it. Contractor licensing is state-specific; some states distinguish hardscape-as-landscape from hardscape-as-construction based on scope or dollar threshold. A contractor-law attorney or the state license board itself can answer this in under a week.' Owner called the state board; found he needed a hardscape-specific sub-license that required a short test and background check. Got it in 60 days before taking any paying hardscape work.
cases.what_wasted_money: A single uninsured hardscape dispute would have been tens of thousands of dollars. Plus license-board fines if caught. The license upgrade cost a few hundred and six weeks.
cases.one_line_lesson: Contractor license categories are state-specific and scope-specific. Adding a new type of work is not the same as accepting a bigger version of existing work — the license question needs to be asked every time the service mix changes.

cases.challenge_pattern: restaurant adding a service (alcohol, catering, outdoor seating) that requires permits the owner didn't know about
cases.observation: Neighborhood cafe adding beer-and-wine service. Owner assumed 'it's just a menu addition, add it to the POS.' The legal category: liquor licensing is state AND municipal, often with waiting periods, zoning requirements, neighborhood-notice rules, and specific employee-training requirements (TIPS or equivalent). In many jurisdictions, selling alcohol without the proper license is a criminal offense, not just a fine, and the owner could personally face charges. Additionally, food-service insurance often excludes liquor liability without a separate rider.
cases.what_worked: Urgency-before-launch: 'alcohol service has a multi-step licensing process that varies by city and state, often takes 60-120 days, and can require neighborhood notification or zoning review. You cannot legally serve before the license is in hand. Separately, your current insurance probably excludes liquor liability — you need a dram-shop rider. Third, your servers need alcohol-server certification, which is a specific short training. Talk to a hospitality-licensing attorney or a restaurant consultant — the owner should not self-navigate this, and the timeline from application to serve-date is months.' Owner planned the rollout over a full 120 days, applied properly, got the rider, trained staff. Service opened on schedule, compliantly.
cases.what_wasted_money: Would have been illegal service (criminal exposure), an uninsured liquor liability event, or both. The licensing process feels slow because it IS slow; there's no legitimate shortcut.
cases.one_line_lesson: Alcohol service is not a menu addition; it's a regulated license category with multi-month timelines, zoning considerations, and specific insurance and training requirements. The process timeline alone means this is planned months ahead, not weeks.

cases.challenge_pattern: restaurant owner misunderstanding tipped-wage rules and overtime calculations in ways that create wage-and-hour liability
cases.observation: Family restaurant paying servers tipped-minimum-wage plus tips, with some servers working 45-50 hours during peak weeks. Owner calculated overtime on tipped-minimum only. Federal law (and many state laws) require overtime on the FULL minimum wage, not the tipped reduced rate — so the overtime-hour calculation was typically 30-40% under-paid. Over a year this was thousands in underpaid wages per server. Wage-and-hour class actions in the restaurant industry are common; this kind of miscalculation is a standard finding.
cases.what_worked: Flagged urgency-medium-become-today-if-raised: 'tipped-wage overtime is a specific calculation that catches many restaurant owners — overtime has to be calculated against the full minimum wage, not the tipped rate. This is worth an employment lawyer or payroll service review immediately. If a former employee files a wage complaint, state labor departments investigate retroactively two to three years typically, with the employer paying back wages plus penalties. A payroll service that knows restaurant rules — Gusto, ADP, a local payroll CPA — handles this automatically.' Owner moved to a restaurant-aware payroll service within 60 days; corrected going forward.
cases.what_wasted_money: Contingent back-wage exposure across all tipped staff for two to three prior years plus penalty doubling if the state labor department found willful underpayment. Switching payroll services cost roughly $100/month.
cases.one_line_lesson: Tipped-wage and overtime rules are specific and unforgiving. Most restaurant wage-and-hour errors aren't malicious; they're from owners who didn't know the formula. A restaurant-aware payroll service removes the risk for roughly $100/month.

cases.challenge_pattern: studio treating regular recurring instructors as 1099 contractors when the working relationship meets the employee test
cases.observation: Boutique yoga studio with eight instructors, all paid as 1099 contractors. Most taught regular weekly slots on the studio's schedule, used the studio's equipment, followed the studio's class format, and were paid per class the studio set. The classification question under IRS and state-DOL tests pointed clearly to employee, not contractor. The instructors mostly didn't care; the exposure was entirely on the studio side — potential retroactive payroll-tax, workers-comp, and unemployment-insurance liability plus state penalties, plus the possibility that one disgruntled former instructor could trigger an audit.
cases.what_worked: Flagged the category and calibrated: 'worker classification for recurring-schedule fitness instructors is a common exposure; many studios operate this way because it's simpler, but the IRS and state-DOL tests don't care about preference — they care about the actual working relationship. This is a talk-to-an-employment-lawyer-this-quarter situation. Options are (a) reclassify to W-2 employees with proper payroll setup; (b) verify classification is defensible with a formal review; (c) restructure relationships with enough operational distance that 1099 classification is defensible.' Owner engaged an employment attorney for a review ($600), which confirmed reclassification was warranted; moved to W-2 over three months via a payroll service. Avoided what would have been a material back-tax exposure.
cases.what_wasted_money: Back-tax liability was growing quietly. Ex-instructor complaints in this industry are common and often trigger state audits.
cases.one_line_lesson: Worker classification isn't a label; it's a test applied to the actual relationship. For recurring-schedule fitness instructors, 1099 classification is often indefensible — and the exposure is retroactive.

cases.challenge_pattern: fitness business with a waiver and insurance policy that aren't aligned — the waiver assumes coverage the insurance doesn't provide
cases.observation: Personal training business in a leased gym space. Owner had a generic client intake waiver. Separately, she had a liability insurance policy from an online broker at $60/month. Owner assumed the policy covered what the waiver released. They didn't align: the policy had specific exclusions for 'equipment-related injury' that required separate coverage for the types of equipment she was using, and the policy required specific waiver language she didn't have. If a client had injured themselves on her equipment, she could have been both unprotected by her waiver AND uncovered by her insurance.
cases.what_worked: Urgency-before-next-client, even if routine: 'your waiver and your insurance policy need to be read together — they each reference the other, and if they don't match, there's a gap your protection depends on. A fitness-industry insurance broker (not a generic broker) can review the policy against your actual activities and often upgrade to a policy that actually covers your work for similar cost. Budget a single annual review with a fitness-aware broker.' Owner switched to a fitness-specific broker, got proper coverage for her equipment-use, and updated her waiver to match policy language. Monthly cost rose $15, coverage became real.
cases.what_wasted_money: Would have been the full liability exposure on an equipment-related injury claim. Generic liability insurance for fitness professionals often excludes the things that actually happen.
cases.one_line_lesson: Waivers and insurance policies reference each other and only work when they're aligned. A cheap generic policy can be worse than no policy if it excludes what the waiver claims to cover.

cases.challenge_pattern: e-commerce site capturing email addresses and customer data across state and country lines without privacy-policy or consent compliance
cases.observation: Small skincare brand with a Shopify site, newsletter signup, and abandoned-cart email sequences. Shipped to all US states and some international. Privacy policy was a generic template; no cookie-consent banner; newsletter signup had no explicit consent mechanism. Legal categories applicable: CCPA (California's privacy law — applies if the business meets revenue thresholds OR processes data for California residents), CAN-SPAM (email marketing), and if any EU customers subscribed, GDPR (strict consent and data-handling rules). She was not compliant with any of them in a material way.
cases.what_worked: Flagged the category without alarm: 'data-privacy compliance is a set of specific obligations that grows as you scale. At your volume the biggest near-term risk is CCPA if you have California customers — check if you meet the thresholds. Cheapest path: a privacy-policy-compliant plugin like Termly or iubenda ($10-40/month) generates a proper policy, adds the cookie banner, and adds explicit consent to your signup forms. More seriously, if you're actively marketing in the EU, GDPR compliance is non-trivial and warrants a privacy-focused attorney review. For now, the first layer.' Owner installed Termly, updated signup consent, added cookie banner; plan to revisit with an attorney at $1M revenue.
cases.what_wasted_money: Current exposure is regulatory-penalty risk (state attorneys general can fine for CCPA violations; class actions exist in some states). Rose with revenue. Compliance cost at this stage: under $500/year.
cases.one_line_lesson: Data-privacy compliance for e-commerce is a scale-progressive obligation. Basic compliance is cheap and plugin-based; full compliance at scale requires a privacy-focused attorney. The moment to start is before revenue triggers state or international thresholds.

cases.challenge_pattern: brand about to scale with a name that's close to an existing registered trademark in an adjacent product category
cases.observation: Small candle brand with a distinctive name about to invest in serious packaging, trademark application, and ad spend. Basic USPTO search (something the owner could have done) would have shown an identical name registered for aromatherapy diffusers two years earlier — adjacent category, same customer, same retail channels. The legal category: trademark infringement risk, plus loss of ability to register their own mark, plus potential cease-and-desist that would require costly rebranding mid-scale.
cases.what_worked: Urgency-today-before-investment: 'a trademark clearance search is a one-time $400-800 cost with a trademark attorney and should happen before you spend money on anything brand-specific — packaging, ads, domain purchases, anything. A basic USPTO search by you is the screen; a formal clearance by an attorney is the confirmation. The category you're worried about is called ''likelihood of confusion'' — the question is whether a typical customer would be confused between your candle and this other company's diffusers, and in the aromatherapy space, that answer is probably yes.' Owner stopped the packaging investment, did a proper clearance (confirmed the conflict), and rebranded before launch. Saved the investment.
cases.what_wasted_money: Would have been $8-20K in packaging, ads, domain, and launch investment plus the cost of a rebrand + the cost of defending or settling an infringement claim. Clearance cost was $600.
cases.one_line_lesson: Trademark clearance is a pre-investment check, not a post-scale afterthought. The moment to do it is before any brand-specific spend — a $600 clearance can save $20K+ in rebrand costs and legal defense.
